channel_name: The Compound and Friends · Josh Brown
video_title: The World You Knew Is Never Coming Back | TCAF 247
gist: Morgan Stanley's Deputy Global Head of Research Michael Zezas argues the post-1980s globalized 'Washington consensus' is permanently over and a multipolar world of reindustrialization, rising trade barriers and defense/industrial CapEx is the durable regime. He layers on an early-innings AI buildout thesis (~$2.9T data-center CapEx 2025-28, ~25% of US GDP growth this year from the build) and is constructive on equities broadening out, developed ex-US, and a productivity payoff that is coming but hard to time.

themes.theme: Regime change: the multipolar world thesis

insights.claim: The pre-2008 'Washington consensus' (free trade, minimal government in the economy, multilateral institutions, US soft power) is permanently over and not reverting, because US voter preferences have shifted against it after 15 years of stagnant wages, opioid crises and wars of choice.
insights.speaker: Michael Zezas
insights.so_what: Frame all macro positioning around durable deglobalization rather than waiting for a reversion to 2005-style conditions; trade barriers and industrial policy are structural, not cyclical.
insights.quote: things are not going back to normal. In fact, what you're seeing is kind of what American voters have elected.

insights.claim: Morgan Stanley's 2020 'multipolar world' call stated that the best benefits of globalization were behind us and geopolitical fragility was rising, requiring investors to assume rising trade barriers and supply-chain fragmentation as a base case.
insights.speaker: Michael Zezas
insights.so_what: A six-year-old, validated framework to anchor portfolio priors: bias toward reshoring, defense and supply-chain resilience beneficiaries.
insights.quote: there's been a regime change driven by shifting voter preferences

insights.claim: The direction of travel on trade barriers (tariff or non-tariff) is up regardless of which US party holds power, since Democrats under Biden kept and expanded Trump-era tariffs rather than rolling them back.
insights.speaker: Michael Zezas
insights.so_what: Don't bet on tariff de-escalation as a regime; investors who assumed Biden would roll back tariffs were wrong, and the same complacency could repeat.

insights.claim: Closing of critical chokepoints (Russia-Ukraine, US-Iran, Strait of Hormuz) is pushing global C-suites toward 'anti-fragile' supply-chain strategies that require years and large capital to retool away from China.
insights.speaker: Michael Zezas
insights.so_what: Multi-year capex tailwind for reshoring/reindustrialization plays and suppliers of factory retooling; a structural, slow-burn investment theme.
insights.quote: we can no longer sort of optimize for globalization. We need what our investment bankers call an anti-fragile strategy.

insights.claim: Industrial policy shows strong bipartisan continuity: the government taking an equity stake in Intel traces directly to the CHIPS Act passed under Biden, so midterm outcomes will be far more noise than signal for policy.
insights.speaker: Michael Zezas
insights.so_what: Policy continuity, not election swings, drives the industrial-policy trade; fade knee-jerk midterm-driven moves.
insights.quote: there's actually a fair amount of continuity across these industrial policy choices

insights.claim: Intel exemplifies the reindustrialization thesis: ridiculed and de-rated by ~20 multiple points to trade like an automaker for committing to North American fabs, then re-rated from roughly $15 to $80 as supply-chain security and AI narratives converged plus a US government stake.
insights.speaker: Josh Brown
insights.so_what: A template for how policy-driven onshoring can violently re-rate a left-for-dead industrial/semiconductor name.

insights.claim: There is a large, persistent spread between the policy uncertainty index and the VIX: elevated geopolitical risk is not transmitting into equity or fixed-income volatility the way it historically did.
insights.speaker: Michael Batnick
insights.so_what: Geopolitical-shock hedges keep failing to pay off; sizing tail hedges off historical correlations may be miscalibrated in this regime.
insights.quote: there is a gigantic spread between the two

insights.claim: For potentially existential events (e.g., Strait of Hormuz closure, World War III), the market's optimal strategy is to assign a very low probability of full follow-through, so even direct US-Iran conflict barely moved equities (VIX maybe hit ~50 for minutes).
insights.speaker: Michael Zezas
insights.so_what: Explains why 'buy when the missiles are in the air' keeps working; positioning for binary catastrophe is usually a losing trade.
insights.quote: maybe sometimes your optimal strategy is to put a really low probability on it actually going all the way through

insights.claim: The White House and the Fed are both attentive to the S&P's 200-day moving average, so policymakers tend to pull back from actions that would trigger a genuine bear market once equities approach a real drawdown.
insights.speaker: Josh Brown
insights.so_what: An implied policy 'put' near key technical levels supports buying dips driven purely by rhetoric rather than realized damage.
insights.quote: I think the White House is aware of the 200-day moving average. I think the Fed is, too.

insights.claim: Tying policy to investing is inherently a multi-variable problem: even perfectly forecasting Liberation Day tariffs would have led investors to slash industrial earnings and multiples and be drastically wrong, because AI-driven earnings growth and multiple expansion overwhelmed the tariff drag.
insights.speaker: Josh Brown
insights.so_what: Nailing a policy call is necessary but not sufficient; the market reaction depends on counterbalancing variables, cautioning against single-factor policy trades.
insights.quote: even if you nail the outcome of a policy... it's a multi-variable problem.

insights.claim: Morgan Stanley estimates the average effective US tariff rate is currently sitting around 12-15%, roughly in line with the firm's pre-2024 base case (which had assumed it kicked in later), versus the ~30% implied on Liberation Day.
insights.speaker: Michael Zezas
insights.so_what: A concrete tariff level to plug into inflation, Fed-policy and earnings models rather than the headline Liberation Day shock numbers.
insights.quote: I think the average effective rate is sitting in like the the sort of like 12 to 15% range now.

insights.claim: Morgan Stanley's economics team (Seth Carpenter) deliberately did not follow competitors into an immediate recession call after the tariff shock because multiple offsetting factors were in play.
insights.speaker: Michael Zezas
insights.so_what: Validates a non-consensus 'no recession' stance; rapid recession calls on policy shocks have been a recurring street error.
insights.quote: A lot of our competitors went straight to recession call

insights.claim: Europe and Japan deciding to reindustrialize, rearm and build their own supply chains rather than rely on the US is one of the era's biggest changes, with positive and underappreciated equity investment implications.
insights.speaker: Josh Brown
insights.so_what: Supports an overweight to developed ex-US equities tied to defense and industrial buildout; overseas stocks already had a strong year.
insights.quote: maybe sometimes what certain country markets need is a little bit of fear and a little bit of paranoia for the animal spirits to get going.

insights.claim: European equities are a 'sneaky' AI beneficiary because the asset-heavy nature of European businesses creates room for meaningful productivity gains from AI adoption.
insights.speaker: Michael Zezas
insights.so_what: An overlooked angle: own European industrials/asset-heavy names not just for rearmament but for an AI productivity kicker.

insights.claim: Japan is more investable on corporate governance reform, rising nationalism and healthy inflation, with a household base holding roughly $7 trillion (about 50%) in deposits that could rotate into risk assets.
insights.speaker: Michael Zezas
insights.so_what: A potential multi-year demand source for Japanese equities if households shift out of cash; distinct catalyst set from Europe.
insights.quote: you've got a household base there that like 50% is invested in deposits. I think the number is about like $7 trillion

themes.theme: AI capex cycle: still early innings

insights.claim: Morgan Stanley projects roughly $2.9 trillion of global data-center CapEx from 2025 to 2028, with the buildout still in early stages relative to hyperscaler compute ambitions.
insights.speaker: Michael Zezas
insights.so_what: Quantifies the runway for AI infrastructure spend; supports continued exposure to enablers despite gains already made.
insights.quote: You guys have an estimate for $2.9 trillion in global CapEx.

insights.claim: Morgan Stanley's economics team attributes about a quarter of this year's US GDP growth to the AI buildout itself (construction, laying pipes, structures), with very little productivity upside yet baked in.
insights.speaker: Michael Zezas
insights.so_what: Macro growth is structurally dependent on AI construction continuing; a slowdown in the build would be a meaningful GDP headwind, and productivity gains are upside not yet priced.
insights.quote: our economics team has about a quarter of GDP growth this year coming from just like the build out of the current build out of AI

insights.claim: The real constraints on the AI buildout are energy and labor (and a bit of policy), which are making it run slower than investors want and therefore keeping it at an earlier stage.
insights.speaker: Michael Zezas
insights.so_what: Power and labor bottlenecks favor energy/utility and infrastructure suppliers and extend the duration of the spend cycle.
insights.quote: the the sort of like governors, the constraints around that build out, which which are energy and labor

insights.claim: AI faces a growing, non-partisan grassroots PR/permitting problem (community pushback in Virginia, California, Oklahoma) that is a risk to watch but not yet large enough to materially constrain data-center siting.
insights.speaker: Michael Zezas
insights.so_what: Monitor local opposition as a slow-developing bottleneck; siting flexibility currently mitigates it but it could escalate.
insights.quote: the concept of AI definitely has like a PR problem

themes.theme: AI earnings reality vs. broadening trade

insights.claim: The share of MSCI World companies reporting tangible AI benefits in financial statements has risen from roughly 20% at the start of last year into the 30s now.
insights.speaker: Michael Zezas
insights.so_what: A trackable diffusion metric; rising adoption supports the eventual earnings-broadening thesis.
insights.quote: the beginning of last year was like 20%. We're up into like the 30s now.

insights.claim: Companies Morgan Stanley mapped as AI adopters delivered roughly 2x faster average EBITDA expansion than non-adopters from 2024 to 2025.
insights.speaker: Michael Zezas
insights.so_what: Concrete evidence AI adoption is already translating into superior earnings growth; a screen for stock selection.
insights.quote: the average EBITDA expansion was was 2x faster than the non-adopters

insights.claim: Consensus 2027 EPS growth for AI infrastructure stocks is ~42% versus ~10% for the S&P 500 and just ~3% for the S&P 500 ex-AI-infrastructure, so the index's earnings story is heavily dependent on AI infra delivering.
insights.speaker: Josh Brown
insights.so_what: Quantifies index concentration risk; if AI-infra earnings disappoint, the broad market's growth profile collapses to ~3%.
insights.quote: the consensus 2027 EPS change for AI infrastructure stocks. Insane, 42%. The S&P 500 at 10% and the S&P 500 XAI infrastructure just 3%.

insights.claim: Morgan Stanley expects the next leg of the bull market to broaden out away from hyperscalers, with the divergence between price-broadening and actual earnings-broadening reconciling over time.
insights.speaker: Michael Zezas
insights.so_what: Argues for positioning in non-mega-cap AI adopters ahead of the broadening rather than crowding the same hyperscaler trade.

insights.claim: Broadening is already underway beneath the surface: the Mag 7 are roughly flat year-to-date while the equal-weight S&P is up ~11%, the Nasdaq 100 up ~19% despite flat mega-caps, and equal-weight tech (RSPT) up ~39%.
insights.speaker: Josh Brown
insights.so_what: Evidence the broadening trade is live; equal-weight and breadth-oriented exposure is outperforming the cap-weighted mega-caps.
insights.quote: equal weight tech, RSPT is the ticker, is up 39% year-to-date.

insights.claim: Morgan Stanley's house view positions equities (US broadening plus secular overseas themes) as the leader while bonds serve mainly as a diversifier delivering coupon-like returns, in a secular growth environment driven by CapEx trends.
insights.speaker: Michael Zezas
insights.so_what: Asset-allocation guidance: favor equities over bonds for total return, hold bonds for diversification not alpha, over the next couple of years.
insights.quote: it's not that bonds are a bad place to be, but they're going to serve more as a diversifier, coupon-like returns

themes.theme: AI's impact on research, productivity and edge

insights.claim: Cross-asset, cross-region collaborative thematic research is exactly what LLMs struggle with, so Morgan Stanley is leaning into it as a durable source of alpha; collaborative work earns more than one standard deviation higher client engagement.
insights.speaker: Michael Zezas
insights.so_what: Human judgment on multi-variable, cross-disciplinary problems is the defensible moat as AI commoditizes single-domain analysis.
insights.quote: what LLMs have a hard time doing... certainly right now giving a generalist a big thematic question that has a lot of variable inputs into it... We we get really low success rate of that.

insights.claim: AI makes previously impossible 'level three' research trivial (e.g., scoring 40,000 bond indenture documents and mapping them to spreads), turning analytical intelligence into table stakes and shifting competitive edge to judgment and prompt skill.
insights.speaker: Michael Zezas
insights.so_what: Large research-org headcount advantages erode; edge moves to data quality, prompting skill and human judgment, with risk of 'research slop' as models overfit to please users.
insights.quote: let's let's score bond indenture documents. Let's do 40,000 of them at a time. Let's map them on the spreads and let's see the opportunities.

insights.claim: Industrializing AI productivity inside large organizations (linking workflows and people) is much slower than the mind-boggling individual productivity gains seen by solo users or startups, so enterprise productivity gains will lag.
insights.speaker: Michael Zezas
insights.so_what: Tempers near-term enterprise productivity expectations; the payoff is coming but the timing inside big firms is uncertain.

insights.claim: Morgan Stanley is not a buyer of the thesis that everyone will write their own software, viewing enterprise SaaS as retaining a moat from its ability to industrialize capabilities individuals cannot replicate, and seeing the recent SaaS selloff as overblown.
insights.speaker: Michael Zezas
insights.so_what: Contrarian support for beaten-down enterprise SaaS names against the 'SaaS apocalypse' narrative, though it will be fundamentally harder.
insights.quote: we're not a buyer of the idea that everyone's just going to write software for themselves.

themes.theme: Politics, the Fed and the macro base case

insights.claim: Prediction markets put Democrats at roughly 80% to take the House and about 50/50 to take both chambers, but even a sweep yields no filibuster-proof or veto-proof majority, so existing tax cuts and tariff authority are not at risk.
insights.speaker: Michael Zezas
insights.so_what: Removes a feared political tail risk; fade any knee-jerk selloff in small-cap/OBBBA tax beneficiaries on a Democratic upset.
insights.quote: you don't have filibuster-proof majority to push anything meaningful through. You definitely don't have the majority to um overcome any veto.

insights.claim: Any market reaction to a surprise Democratic midterm sweep (e.g., weakness in small caps tied to OBBBA tax benefits) should be faded, because a tax change is at least three years and a presidential election away.
insights.speaker: Michael Zezas
insights.so_what: Explicit tradeable instruction: fade knee-jerk midterm-driven equity moves.
insights.quote: if they did, I would say fade that

insights.claim: Morgan Stanley clients are 'fine' with Kevin Warsh and the new Fed because he is traditional enough; far more anxiety would have followed an unorthodox or politically perceived pick, though Fed credibility wouldn't have been substantially compromised regardless given the committee structure.
insights.speaker: Michael Zezas
insights.so_what: Reduces near-term Fed-independence tail risk for bonds; the committee structure buffers against a single chair's politicization.
insights.quote: there would be I think far more anxiety if you'd gone with one of these less traditional names

insights.claim: Morgan Stanley's base case for the rest of the year is constructive (not the most bullish on the street) on most asset classes, buoyed by secular CapEx/AI and multipolar-transition themes, contingent on earnings delivering, no worse Middle East escalation, and a stable rate picture.
insights.speaker: Josh Brown
insights.so_what: A clear, conditional roadmap: stay constructive while monitoring earnings, Middle East risk and rates as the key swing factors.
insights.quote: we should finish out the year in pretty good shape
